paragraphs.text: A key caveat: EDTA’s **effective** chelation depends strongly on **pH**, because the free ligand is protonated at lower pH. SRD-46 reports these protonation constants at 25 °C, I = 0.1 M:

claims.text: because the free ligand is protonated at lower pH.

paragraphs.text:
| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Is EDTA a good chelator? | **Yes — strongly and broadly so.** |
| Why? | It is a multidentate aminopolycarboxylate ligand with very broad metal coverage in SRD-46. |
| Main limitation | Its apparent binding strength drops at lower pH because protonation competes with metal binding. |

claims.text: | Is EDTA a good chelator? | **Yes — strongly and broadly so.** |
| Why? | It is a multidentate aminopolycarboxylate ligand with very broad metal coverage in SRD-46. |
| Main limitation | Its apparent binding strength drops at lower pH because protonation competes with metal binding. |

paragraphs.text: Chemistry insight: EDTA is especially useful because it is **hexadentate**, so it benefits from the **chelate effect** and often forms very stable 1:1 metal complexes. In practice, it is excellent for sequestering many metal ions, though selectivity varies by metal and conditions.

claims.text: often forms very stable 1:1 metal complexes
it is excellent for sequestering many metal ions
selectivity varies by metal and conditions

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.
Subjective usefulness is not captured in SRD-46 rows; no entry states EDTA is “especially useful.”
EDTA’s hexadentate nature is standard coordination chemistry; the database does not explicitly annotate denticity.
